title: MAAMOUL Tutorial
text: MAAMOUL requires three main inputs (described ): global metabolic network. P-values enzymes (ECs) gene families. P-values metabolites. p-values derived metabolomic data, indicating association metabolite’s abundance phenotype interest. tutorial, load example datasets provided package, based data Franzosa et al., Nature Microbiology, 2019. also describe users can generate inputs.

title: MAAMOUL Tutorial
text: global metabolic network defines relationships metabolites enzymes. can derived databases like KEGG MetaCyc. network represented table edges connecting metabolite nodes EC nodes. can provided data frame pre-loaded R path .csv file. network bipartite, meaning edges connect nodes different types (metabolites ECs, ECs ECs metabolites metabolites). tutorial, use pre-compiled network (automatically loaded package): Note: row table represents edge; first two columns table used; additional columns ignored. Column names ignored well.

previous_headings: 1. Preparing input data > 1.1 Global metabolic network
title: MAAMOUL Tutorial
text: Users can construct network databases KEGG (https://www.genome.jp/kegg/), MetaCyc (https://metacyc.org/), resources. building custom network, recommend: Removing small connected components; Removing nodes extremely high degree (hub nodes); → can dramatically influence network topology. Especially important currency metabolites (e.g., water, ATP) connect many reactions may informative specific biological question. Filtering irrelevant ECs metabolites based prior knowledge; → e.g., ECs linked bacteria studying gut microbiome.

previous_headings: 1. Preparing input data > 1.1 Global metabolic network
title: MAAMOUL Tutorial
text: better understand network, useful inspect: Node degree distribution Number size connected components Total number nodes edges
code: g <- graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = FALSE)  # Degree distribution deg <- degree(g) ggplot(data.frame(deg), aes(x = deg)) +   geom_histogram(color = 'black', fill = 'grey50', bins = 20) +   ggtitle("Node degree distribution") +   xlab('Node degree') +   ylab('Count') +   scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +   theme_bw() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 11, face = "bold")) # Connected components comp <- components(g) sprintf("Number of connected components: %i", comp$no) #> [1] "Number of connected components: 13" sprintf("Largest component size: %i", max(comp$csize)) #> [1] "Largest component size: 4439" sprintf("Smallest component size: %i", min(comp$csize)) #> [1] "Smallest component size: 10"  # Basic stats sprintf("Number of EC nodes: %i", sum(grepl("^EC", V(g)$name))) #> [1] "Number of EC nodes: 2172" sprintf("Number of metabolite nodes: %i", sum(!grepl("^EC", V(g)$name))) #> [1] "Number of metabolite nodes: 2539" sprintf("Number of edges: %i", ecount(g)) #> [1] "Number of edges: 6253"

title: MAAMOUL Tutorial
text: second input table p-values enzyme (EC) functions (pre-loaded R saved .tsv file). p-values based analysis metagenomic metatranscriptomic data, reflecting association EC’s abundance phenotype interest (e.g., disease status). usually computed using differential abundance analysis methods (e.g., MaAsLin3, ALDEx2, ANCOM). Load pre-computed EC p-values: Notes: EC identifiers must match given global network table; P-value may represent associations directions (enriched depleted phenotype interest), single direction (using one-sided statistical tests). pre-computed example, p-values two-sided, representing association disease. table include least following columns: feature → EC identifier (must match network node names), pval → statistical significance association phenotype, multiple testing correction. Additional columns (e.g., effect size, adjusted p-value) can included required.

title: MAAMOUL Tutorial
text: Similarly, MAAMOUL requires p-values metabolites, derived metabolomic data. Metabolite identifiers must match node names metabolic network.

title: MAAMOUL Tutorial
text: useful inspect well observed features overlap network. observed features missing network, consider building custom network better captures observed features.
code: all_net_nodes <- unique(c(edges[[1]], edges[[2]]))  sprintf(   "%i of %i observed EC features (features with a p-value) are included in the network.",   sum(ec_pvals$feature %in% all_net_nodes),   nrow(ec_pvals) ) #> [1] "1023 of 1597 observed EC features (features with a p-value) are included in the network."  sprintf(   "%i of %i observed metabolite features (features with a p-value) are included in the network.",   sum(mtb_pvals$feature %in% all_net_nodes),   nrow(mtb_pvals) ) #> [1] "143 of 237 observed metabolite features (features with a p-value) are included in the network."

title: MAAMOUL Tutorial
text: input data prepared, MAAMOUL can run identify metabolic modules (subnetworks) include metagenomic-based ECs metabolomic-based metabolites associated phenotype interest. step may take minutes run. real analyses, may take longer (e.g., tens minutes), depending size network, number repeats (N_REPEATS; controls many times randomly assign p-values unobserved nodes), number permutations (N_VAL_PERM; controls number times p-values permuted across network nodes, evaluate significance identified modules). tutorial use N_VAL_PERM = 9 keep runtime short. However, real analyses, strongly recommend using much larger number permutations (e.g., ≥99 ), order obtain reliable estimate modules’ significance. See ?maamoul details parameters. Results found test_outputs directory (explore outputs next section):
code: res <- maamoul(   global_network_edges = edges,   ec_pvals = ec_pvals,   metabolite_pvals = mtb_pvals,   out_dir = "test_outputs",   N_REPEATS = 100,   N_VAL_PERM = 9,   N_THREADS = 2 ) #> Warning: package 'graph' was built under R version 4.4.2 #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Output directory "test_outputs" already exists. Files may be overriden. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Working directory is: /Users/em2035/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-UniversityofCambridge/Documents/GitHub/MAAMOUL/vignettes. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Starting module-identification pipeline. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Note that 63 duplicated metabolites were identified and only the ones with minimal p-values are kept. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Note that 29 duplicated ECs were identified and only the ones with minimal p-values are kept. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Loaded network information and feature p-values. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] 99 of 174 observed metabolite features are also in the network. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] 1004 of 1568 observed EC features are also in the network. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] 1103 of 4711 network nodes are observed in the data. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Metabolite p-value threshold based on BUM: 0.1989. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] EC p-value threshold based on BUM: 0.0461. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Found 255 EC anchor nodes and 69 metabolite anchor nodes. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Constructed a node-weighted network, with 4711 nodes and 6253 edges. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:00] Starting graph random coloring iterations #> .INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:26] End of graph random coloring iterations #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:26] Identified a total of 41 modules (before significance testing). #>   |                                                                              |                                                                      |   0%INFO [2026-03-27 01:09:29] Starting graph random coloring iterations - permuted graphs. #>   |                                                                              |========                                                              |  11%  |                                                                              |================                                                      |  22%  |                                                                              |=======================                                               |  33%  |                                                                              |===============================                                       |  44%  |                                                                              |=======================================                               |  56%  |                                                                              |===============================================                       |  67%  |                                                                              |======================================================                |  78%  |                                                                              |==============================================================        |  89%  |                                                                              |======================================================================| 100%INFO [2026-03-27 01:11:51] Finished graph random coloring iterations - permuted graphs. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:11:51] Computed modules' significance. #> INFO [2026-03-27 01:11:52] Done! print("MAAMOUL run completed. Results written to 'test_outputs/'.") #> [1] "MAAMOUL run completed. Results written to 'test_outputs/'." list.files("test_outputs") #> [1] "anchors_dendogram.svg"        "bum_parameters.csv"           #> [3] "complete_modules.csv"         "ec_bum_fit.png"               #> [5] "graph_and_data.rdata"         "modules_overview.csv"         #> [7] "mtb_bum_fit.png"              "true_vs_permuted_modules.png"

title: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks
text: MAAMOUL knowledge-based computational method integrates metagenomic metabolomic data identify custom data-driven microbial metabolic modules associated disease states. Unlike traditional statistical approaches, MAAMOUL leverages prior biological knowledge bacterial metabolism link genes metabolites global, microbiome-wide metabolic network, projects genes’ metabolites’ disease-association scores onto network. identified ‘modules’ sub-networks graph significantly enriched disease-associated features, metagenomic metabolomic. details see: Muller E, Baum S, Borenstein E. “MAAMOUL: Metabolic network-based discovery microbiome-metabolome shifts disease” preparation.

title: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks
text: MAAMOUL can installed directly GitHub, running following: Note: MAAMOUL package dependent installation ‘BioNet’ package [1]. See installation instructions .

title: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks
code: # Load package library(MAAMOUL)  # Load pre-compiled data tables data(edges) data(ec_pvals) data(mtb_pvals)  # Run MAAMOUL maamoul(global_network_edges = edges,   ec_pvals = ec_pvals,   metabolite_pvals = mtb_pvals,   out_dir = 'test_outputs',   N_REPEATS = 100,   N_VAL_PERM = 9,   N_THREADS = 4 )

what: Tutorial - Running MAAMOUL on your own data
title: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks
text: 📘 Full tutorial: https://borenstein-lab.github.io/MAAMOUL/articles/maamoul_tutorial.html

what: What does it mean if MAAMOUL identified a module but it did not come out significant?
title: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks
text: MAAMOUL first identifies significant nodes (.e., ECs metabolites significantly associated phenotype) grouped closely global metabolic network. termed ‘modules’. p-value computed per module describe likelihood identifying module given topology network specific dataset un hand (e.g., number significant ECs, number significant metabolites, etc.). Intuitively, non-significant p-value means identified module resulted data characteristics random chance rather actual biological signal.

what: MAAMOUL has identified a microbiome-metabolome module in my data, however I see there are additional significant nodes (ECs/metabolite) in proximity to the identified module. Why weren’t these nodes added to the identified module?
title: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks
text: likely, significant nodes weren’t included identified module initially assigned another module, eventually meet module significance threshold. Future method versions include option post-processing modules, potentially merging nearby nodes long modules’ significance isn’t harmed. questions pipeline, please open issue (https://github.com/borenstein-lab/MAAMOUL/issues) contact Prof. Elhanan Borenstein elbo@tauex.tau.ac.il. References Beisser D, Klau GW, Dandekar T, Mueller T, Dittrich M (2010). “BioNet: R-package Functional Analysis Biological Networks.” Bioinformatics, 26(8), 1129-1130.

title: MAAMOUL: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks — maamoul
code: maamoul(   global_network_edges,   ec_pvals,   metabolite_pvals,   out_dir,   SEED = 710,   NODE_FDR_THRESHOLD = 0.1,   N_REPEATS = 1000,   MAX_DIST_BTWN_NODES = 4,   HCLUST_METHOD = "average",   CUTREE_H = 0.8,   MIN_MOD_SIZE = 3,   MIN_ECS_IN_MOD = 0,   MIN_METS_IN_MOD = 0,   N_VAL_PERM = 99,   MODULE_FDR_THRESHOLD = 0.2,   N_THREADS = 1 )

title: MAAMOUL: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks — maamoul
text: global_network_edges path file holding list edges included global metabolic network. file comma- delimited, first column listing EC's second column listing metabolites. Additional columns, exits, ignored. row table indicates edge EC metabolite. table already loaded R, can also directly provided data frame. ec_pvals path file holding metagenomic EC features corresponding p-values representing association disease. FDR corrected. file tab-delimited, column named 'feature' holding EC codes format global network file, 'pval' column holding p-values. columns ignored. table already loaded R, can also directly provided data frame. metabolite_pvals Similar `ec_pvals` file, listing metabolite p-values. Metabolite codes/names format global network file. table already loaded R, can also directly provided data frame. out_dir folder output files saved. SEED integer used seed result reproducibility. NODE_FDR_THRESHOLD FDR threshold determine nodes treated 'anchors' (.e. estimated disease-associated). Default: 0.1. N_REPEATS number random coloring nodes perform. MAX_DIST_BTWN_NODES maximal distance nodes considered taking part disease-associated module. Default: 4. HCLUST_METHOD Either 'average', 'single' 'complete'. Default: 'average'. See `?hclust`. CUTREE_H height hierarchical tree cut determine clusters. MIN_MOD_SIZE minimal size module outputted. Default: 3. MIN_ECS_IN_MOD Modules less number EC nodes discarded. Default: 0. MIN_METS_IN_MOD Modules less number metabolite nodes discarded. Default: 0. N_VAL_PERM Number node-weight permutations perform calculating significance module. MODULE_FDR_THRESHOLD FDR threshold determine modules significant. N_THREADS Number threads use parallel computing. Verify sufficient number cores `parallel::detectCores()` first.

title: MAAMOUL: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks — maamoul
text: method outputs several tables plots `out_dir` folder.

title: MAAMOUL: A method for detecting microbiome-metabolome alterations in disease using metabolic networks — maamoul
code: if (FALSE) { data(edges)  data(ec_pvals)  data(mtb_pvals) maamoul(   global_network_edges = edges,   ec_pvals = ec_pvals,   metabolite_pvals = mtb_pvals,   out_dir = 'test_outputs',   N_REPEATS = 100,   N_VAL_PERM = 9,   N_THREADS = 2   ) }
